coredata.dc:title: Sensitive detection of Sudan dyes using tire-derived carbon dots as a fluorescent sensor
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Yaoping
Gao, Zhijin
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we exploit an innovative strategy to reuse waste rubber tires as a low-cost source for the fabrication of a high-value material, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). In the hydrothermal condition, ammonium persulphate is utilized to oxidize the tires and offer nitrogen atom for doping, to produce CDs with a high quantum yield (QY) of up to 23.8%. Such a QY is outstanding among the reported waste-derived CDs. It is found that the fluorescence of CDs can be remarkably quenched by Sudan I–IV with negligible interference from other substances. The corresponding linear ranges are 0.5–60, 0.5–60, 1–70, and 1–70 μM, and limits of detection are 0.17, 0.21, 0.53, and 0.62 μM for Sudan I, II, III, and IV, respectively. Systematic investigations reveal that the fluorescence quenching mainly stems from the inner filter effect. Moreover, the CD-based sensor shows an excellent applicability for the assay of Sudan dyes in chili powder sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Waste rubber tires
Fluorescence detection
Sudan dyes
Inner filter effect

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs from waste rubber tires
Measurement of Sudan dyes in the real sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) size distribution, (c) XRD pattern, and (d) Raman spectrum of the CDs prepared from waste tires. Inset in (a): high-resolution TEM image of CDs.
(a) XPS survey, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) FTIR spectra of the CDs prepared from waste tires.

(a) UV–vis absorption and (b) PL spectra of the CDs prepared from waste tires. Insets in (a): Photographs of the CD suspension in daylight and under a UV lamp (365 nm). Inset in (b): normalized PL spectra of CDs with excitation of 300–460 nm.

(a) PL spectra of CDs at 360 nm excitation upon addition of different concentrations of Sudan I (from top to bottom: 0, 0.5, 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, and 80 μM). (b) The fluorescence quenching efficiency (I 0–I)/I 0 as a function of the concentration of Sudan I. (c) and (d) the selectivity of CDs toward Sudan dyes against common ions and potential organic interferents. All the concentrations of analytes are 60 μM. I and I 0 refer to the fluorescence intensities of CDs at the peak position with and without analyte.
(a) Normalized PL spectrum of CDs at 360 nm excitation and UV–vis absorption spectrum of Sudan I. (b) PL decay profiles of CDs with and without Sudan I.
A diagram about the synthesis of CDs from waste rubber tires and their application for sensing of Sudan dyes.
Results for detection of Sudan I–IV in chili powder using the CD sensor.

head.ce:title: Sensitive detection of Sudan dyes using tire-derived carbon dots as a fluorescent sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we exploit an innovative strategy to reuse waste rubber tires as a low-cost source for the fabrication of a high-value material, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). In the hydrothermal condition, ammonium persulphate is utilized to oxidize the tires and offer nitrogen atom for doping, to produce CDs with a high quantum yield (QY) of up to 23.8%. Such a QY is outstanding among the reported waste-derived CDs. It is found that the fluorescence of CDs can be remarkably quenched by Sudan I–IV with negligible interference from other substances. The corresponding linear ranges are 0.5–60, 0.5–60, 1–70, and 1–70 μM, and limits of detection are 0.17, 0.21, 0.53, and 0.62 μM for Sudan I, II, III, and IV, respectively. Systematic investigations reveal that the fluorescence quenching mainly stems from the inner filter effect. Moreover, the CD-based sensor shows an excellent applicability for the assay of Sudan dyes in chili powder sample.

ce:section.ce:para: Sudan I, II, III, and IV, containing chromophoric azo groups, are extensively used in chemical industry for colouring hydrocarbon solvents, oils, waxes, and also serve as additives in some foodstuffs to improve the appearance, owing to their favorite color and good stability [1]. However, the uptake of Sudan dyes in a human body may cause cancers, so they are now prohibited in the food production [2]. Many analytical methods have been developed for the detection of Sudan dyes, including high performance liquid chromatography [3], mass spectrometry [4,5], electrochemistry technique [6], Raman approach [7], and fluorescence assay [8–11]. But a majority of methods require sophisticated equipment, expensive materials, complicate procedures, and/or time-consuming processes, which restrain their wide applications. Hence, the set-up of a cost-effective, convenient, and rapid method for accurate detection of Sudan dyes is of great importance.

Carbon dots (CDs), one of the most popular representatives of photoluminescent carbonaceous materials, hold promising applications in diverse areas that range from bioimaging to sensing, catalysis, and light emitting diode, due to their unique properties such as strong fluorescence, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and facile functionalization [12–16]. The past several years have seen the emergence of numerous synthetic methods for preparing CDs from various precursors [17–19]. In the perspective of saving resource, reducing cost and relieving environmental burden, the utilization of man-made wastes like frying oil [20] and combustion soot [21–23] as feedstock toward CDs is attractive, because these wastes are cheap and readily available while CD fabrication provides a new way for waste treatment and recycling. Unfortunately, these cases suffer from the requirement of corrosive mineral acids. Moreover, the waste-derived CDs possess low photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY), typically less than 5%, which need to be further improved. At present, the recycling of man-made wastes toward highly bright CDs has become a vital research demand.

Here we address the reuse of waste tires as an alternative carbon source for the economical preparation of CDs (Scheme 1 ). With the assistance of ammonium persulphate in the hydrothermal condition, the waste tires comprising rubber components went through oxidative degradation, polymerization, and aromatization, to eventually become strongly photoluminescent CDs with a QY of up to 23.8%. It was found that the resultant CDs exhibited a remarkable fluorescence decrease upon addition of Sudan I–IV based on the inner filter effect (IFE), which made them good sensors for the detection of the hazardous dyes. In addition, the applicability of CDs for the assay of Sudan dyes in chili powder sample achieved satisfactory results.

Waste rubber tire was received from a repair shop in the city of Ningbo. It was cleaned with water and ethanol, and then cut into small pieces for further use. Ammonium persulphate was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glucose, fructose, sucrose, maltose, cysteine, lysine, glutamic acid, ascorbic acid, urea, citric acid, rhodamine B, and congo red were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs from waste rubber tires
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of waste rubber tires in the presence of ammonium persulphate. In a typical synthesis, 0.2 g of small tire pieces and 1.0 g of ammonium persulphate were put into a 50-mL autoclave containing 30 mL of deionized water. Then, the mixture was heated in an oven at 220 °C for 16 h. After cooling, a yellow suspension of CDs was obtained. The purification of CDs was conducted through centrifugation of the suspension at 8000 rpm for 5 min to remove the residues, followed by dialysis against deionized water using a cellulose membrane (molecular weight cut off: 1000 Da) for 8 times. Finally, about 8 mg of the CD powder was obtained by freeze-drying.

The detection procedures were measured as follows: 70 mg of the CD powder was dispersed in 1000 mL of deionized water to form a 70 mg L−1 suspension. Then, 900 μL of the Sudan I–IV ethanol solution with different concentrations was mixed with 100 μL of the above CD suspension. After 1 min, the fluorescence emission spectrum of the mixture was recorded under the excitation of 360 nm. Selectivity experiments were carried out as same as for Sudan dyes.

Chili powder was used as a real sample for the assay of Sudan dyes. The pretreatment procedures were according to the method of National Standard of the People's Republic of China (2005) [11]. In detail, 0.6 g of chili powder was put into a 100-mL beaker containing 30 mL of ethanol. After 10 min stirring, 30 min sonication, and 20 min precipitation, the supernatant fluid was collected and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 6 min. The final eluate was heated to remove the organic solution. Then, 400 μL of the pretreated sample was mixed with 500 μL of the standard solution of Sudan dyes (0, 20, 40 μM) and 100 μL of the CD suspension. The fluorescence measurement was performed as same as above. All the tests were carried out in triplicate.

Waste tires typically consist of natural rubber, synthetic polyisoprene, butadiene rubber, styrene-butadiene rubber, and carbon black [24], so the main elements in tires are C and H. Commonly, CDs contain an additional element of O, which exists in the form of oxygenic functional groups like hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl. Thus, for the purpose of converting waste tires to CDs, a suitable oxygen source should be supplemented. Improving the QY of CDs is another consideration. Nitrogen doping is an effective way, as the N atom injects electrons into CDs, leading to the alteration of electronic environment and enhancement of PL efficiency [25]. With the above goals in mind, ammonium persulphate (NH4)2S2O8 was selected as both oxygen and nitrogen sources. At high hydrothermal temperature, the S2O8 2− ion decomposed to release O2, which facilitated the oxidative degradation of rubber tires, producing a large amount of small oxidized fragments. The NH4 + ion then reacted with the oxidized fragments, introducing the nitrogen atom. After that, the above-formed N-containing fragments proceeded polymerization and aromatization to finally convert into N-doped CDs.

Hydrothermal treatment of tire debris in the (NH4)2S2O8 solution at 220 °C for 16 h brought about a yellow dispersion of CDs. As seen in the wide-area TEM image (Fig. 1a), the resultant CDs have a nearly spherical morphology and are individually distributed without aggregation. They exhibit a size range of 4–11 nm with an average diameter of about 7.5 nm, fitting well to the Gaussian function (Fig. 1b). The high-resolution TEM image of a representative dot (inset of Fig. 1a) reveals the amorphous structure of the nanocarbon, because no obvious lattice fringe is found. The XRD pattern of CDs (Fig. 1c) shows a broad diffraction peak centred at 23.1°, which is attributed to the (002) plane of disordered carbons, suggesting their poor crystallinity [26]. The low crystal quality of CDs is also proved by the Raman spectrum (Fig. 1d), where the disorder-induced D band has a strong intensity, comparable to that of graphitic G band, implying that CDs possess many sp3 defects [27].

The XPS survey spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2a) shows the presence of C1s, N1s, and O1s signals with atomic concentrations of 66.69%, 6.62%, and 26.69% respectively, suggesting that CDs are mainly composed of C and O, and also contain a small quantity of N. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) can be deconvoluted into four components: a predominant peak at 284.5 eV corresponding to the graphitic C, and other three weaker peaks at 285.6, 286.5, and 287.9 eV assigned to CN, CO, and C=O/C=N bonds respectively [28]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) reflects different N species of pyridinic N at 398.5 eV, pyrrolic N at 399.5 eV, and graphitic N at 400.5 eV, implying that N atoms has been introduced into the carbon skeleton of CDs [29]. The curve fitting of the O1s spectrum (Fig. S1) displays the subpeaks of CO at 531.6 eV and CO at 532.7 eV, which indicates that CDs are decorated with oxygenous groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl [30]. FTIR characterization was measured to further obtain the structural information of CDs. Distinctive bands are illustrated in Fig. 2d, including the stretching vibrations of OH at 3373 cm−1, CH at 2943 and 2870 cm−1, CO at 1655 cm−1, aromatic C=C/C=N at 1539 and 1439 cm−1, CN at 1248 cm−1, and CO at 1063 cm−1. In addition, the out-plane CH ring-structure deformation at 611 cm−1 and CH bending vibration at 1348 cm−1 are observed. The FTIR interpretations agree well with the XPS results, revealing that CDs consist of aromatic clusters and are surrounded by abundant oxygenous groups.

Fig. 3a displays the optical absorption characteristics of the CD suspension. There is an inconspicuous shoulder at 286 nm, corresponding to the n–π* transitions of carbonyl/carboxyl moieties on the CD surface [31]. In the visible region of 400–800 nm, the absorption is rather weak, which gives the CD suspension a pale color of yellow. Under a UV lamp irradiation, CDs emit a brilliant blue fluorescence (inset of Fig. 3a). Fig. 3b presents the excitation dependent PL behavior of CDs. The maximum emission peak is located at 382 nm with 320 nm excitation. Increasing the excitation wavelength from 320 to 460 nm leads to the red-shift of emission position and the reduction of intensity. This phenomenon is common in fluorescent carbon materials, possibly originating from the different particle sizes in the sample and diverse emissive species on each dot [32]. The PL decay curve of CDs (Fig. S2) can be described by a double exponential function, giving a fast component τ1 of 2.97 ns and a slow component τ2 of 11.61 ns, which are assigned to the recombination processes in the inner carbon core and outer functionalized surface, respectively [33]. The proportion of τ1 (A1) is 51.54%, slightly larger than that of τ2 (A2, 48.46%). According to the equation of τaverage = (A1τ1 2 + A2τ2 2) / (A1τ1 + A2τ2), the average PL lifetime is calculated to be 9.76 ns. The as-prepared CDs achieve a PL QY of 23.8% by comparison with quinine sulfate, much higher than the most value of waste-derived CDs (Table S1), probably due to the successful nitrogen doping and good surface passivation. It should be mentioned that (NH4)2S2O8, which not only acts as an oxidizing agent but also provides nitrogen atom, is vital in the synthesis of highly bright CDs from waste rubber tires. If (NH4)2S2O8 is replaced by Na2S2O8 or H2O2, the resultant CDs is nitrogen free, showing weaker fluorescence with QYs below 10%.

Inspired by their unique PL properties, the practicality of using the bright CDs to detect Sudan dyes was evaluated based on fluorescence quenching. The sensing of Sudan I was taken as a typical example. As shown in Figs. S3–S5, the effects of pH, CD concentration, and reaction time on the sensing performance were investigated. Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence quenching efficiency (I 0–I)/I 0 at 360 nm excitation reached a value of 52% upon addition of 50 μM of Sudan I, indicating that a trace amount of the analyte could significantly decrease the fluorescence of the probe. Fig. 4a illustrates the PL spectra of CDs in the presence of different concentrations of Sudan I, where the fluorescence intensity gradually declines with the increased concentration of the analyte. The linear detection range is estimated to be from 0.5 to 60 μM, and the regression equation can be expressed as (I 0–I)/I 0 = 0.0103 × C Sudan I (μM) – 0.0010 with a correlation coefficient R 2 of 0.99 (Fig. 4b). According to the rule of 3σ/k, the limit of detection (LOD) of CDs toward Sudan I is determined to be 0.17 μM. Similarly, for detection of Sudan II, III, and IV (Figs. S6–S8), the linear ranges are 0.5–60, 1–70, and 1–70 μM, with LODs of 0.21, 0.53, and 0.62 μM, respectively.

The sensing selectivity was examined. Various common ions, including Ag+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, F−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, and SO4 2−, have negligible quenching effects on the fluorescence intensity of CDs (Fig. 4c). Some possible compounds in food, such as glucose, fructose, sucrose, maltose, cysteine, lysine, glutamic acid, ascorbic acid, urea, and citric acid, do not cause apparent fluorescence fluctuations of CDs under the same detection conditions. The dyes like congo red and rhodamine B can quench the fluorescence of CDs. However, these dyes typically do not exist in food, so their interference can be ruled out. The above results demonstrate the excellent sensing specificity of CDs toward Sudan dyes. The comparison of our method with previous approaches is presented in Table S2. Our method has the combined advantage of high sensitivity and low cost in material.

In order to understand the sensing mechanism, systematic investigations were done. The TEM image (Fig. S9) shows that no aggregation takes place after addition of Sudan I in the CD solution, which excludes the aggregation-induced quenching mechanism [34,35]. As depicted in Fig. 5a, the emission spectrum of CDs has a large overlap with the absorption band of Sudan I, indicating that the fluorescence quenching may be caused by the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) [36]. Time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy was carried out to distinguish the aforementioned two mechanisms. After addition of Sudan I, the average fluorescence lifetime of CDs displays an approximately constant value from 9.76 to 9.63 ns (Fig. 5b), suggesting that the primary sensing mechanism is IFE rather than FRET [37]. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching efficiency is temperature-independent (Fig. S10), and the shape of emission line changes with the appearance of new peaks in the region of 520–650 nm by adding enough Sudan dyes (Figs. 4a and S6–S8), which are also evidences of IFE [9]. In the same concentration of Sudan I–IV, the fluorescence quenching efficiency is Sudan I > II > III > IV (Fig. 4c), due to the corresponding decreased overlap area between the emission spectrum of fluorophore and absorption band of quencher (Fig. S11). This phenomenon further proves the mechanism of IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of Sudan dyes in the real sample
ce:section.ce:para: To test its real applicability, the CD sensor was employed to analyze Sudan I–IV in chili powder according to the procedures described in the Experimental section. As expected, no Sudan dyes were detectable in the chili sample. Using the standard addition method, the recoveries ranged from 92.5% to 106.9%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were less than 5.1% (Table 1 ), which suggested that the proposed method was reliable for the assay of Sudan dyes in the actual food samples.

In summary, highly fluorescent CDs are prepared by hydrothermal treatment of waste rubber tires in the presence of ammonium persulphate. The obtained CDs can be used to detect Sudan dyes based on fluorescence quenching through IFE. This analytical method has several advantages: (1) The fluorescent sensor, made from waste material, is low-cost. (2) It is sensitive. The LODs are 0.17, 0.21, 0.53, and 0.62 μM for Sudan I, II, III, and IV, respectively. (3) The fluorescence method is fast. The response time is only 1 min. (4) The detection method shows good selectivity. The proposed method has been successfully applied in the detection of Sudan dyes in chili powder. This work offers a possible route for facile conversion of waste to bright CDs targeting fluorescence analysis of harmful dyes in food.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Yaoping Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Software, Funding acquisition, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Zhijin Gao: Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LQ20E020005), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City (2019A610188), the State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS2004), and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118514.

Mater., 24 (2012) 2037–2041.
